does: This paper conducts the first comprehensive empirical evaluation of four reward model variants across 14 diverse domains to determine which best assesses LLM reasoning during test-time scaling. The authors compare discriminative and generative versions of both outcome reward models (ORMs, which score final answers) and process reward models (PRMs, which score intermediate steps). Contrary to prior consensus favoring PRMs, they find that generative ORMs (GenORM) consistently outperform other variants across all tested domains. The work reveals that discriminative ORMs match discriminative PRMs despite being simpler, generative PRMs fail to be competitive, and the robustness of GenORM stems from avoiding the compounding label noise and trajectory-length challenges that plague stepwise PRM scoring.
connects: This work directly challenges the dominant narrative in recent test-time scaling research, which has emphasized PRMs as superior to ORMs based primarily on evidence from mathematical reasoning tasks. The paper builds on growing interest in using external verifiers to improve LLM reliability and extends beyond prior narrow evaluations by testing across diverse domains. It responds to the limitations of previous auto-labeling approaches used to train PRMs, which borrowed techniques from process supervision in reinforcement learning. The evaluation methodology combines insights from both discriminative and generative modeling approaches to reward learning, connecting to broader debates about how to best utilize LLM-generated supervision and the generalization of reward models across different reasoning types.
enables: These findings open new directions for efficient test-time scaling by suggesting that simpler outcome-based reward models can achieve comparable or better performance than more complex process-level alternatives. The discovery that stepwise scoring accumulates noise from auto-labeling creates an opportunity for research into denoising techniques or alternative ways to incorporate intermediate reasoning information. The robustness of generative ORMs across diverse domains motivates investigation into why generative modeling provides greater stability and generalization than discriminative approaches. Future work can explore whether GenORM gains extend to specialized domains, how to further improve performance by combining outcome and process information without the label noise problems, and whether these insights apply to other test-time scaling methods like tree search or beam search with multiple candidate trajectories.
